explain: HIPAA compliance for analytics requires: (1) Audit logging — Redshift audit logging captures all SQL queries with user/timestamp to S3 for 6+ year retention. (2) Encryption — KMS CMKs for both S3 and Redshift satisfy the encryption at rest requirement. (3) Minimum necessary — Lake Formation column-level access ensures users only see PHI relevant to their role (e.g., billing team sees patient IDs but not diagnosis codes). (4) AWS BAA covers the PHI processing under HIPAA. Macie detects PHI but doesn't enforce access control or provide query-level audit logs. DynamoDB with client-side encryption avoids standard data lake tooling unnecessarily. Cognito handles authentication but doesn't provide Redshift query audit logs.

q: A data platform team needs to replicate data changes from an operational Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database to a data lake in Amazon S3 in near-real-time (latency <1 minute). The solution must capture inserts, updates, and deletes without impacting the RDS database's write performance. Which service is purpose-built for this?
options: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with change data capture (CDC) mode — DMS reads the PostgreSQL WAL (Write Ahead Log) without impacting write performance and delivers changes to Amazon S3 (or Kinesis) in near-real-time
Create an RDS event subscription that triggers a Lambda function on each database modification, with Lambda writing changes to S3
Use AWS Glue with JDBC connection to poll the RDS database every minute for changes using a timestamp column, writing new/updated records to S3
Enable RDS automated backups and use S3 event notifications to trigger a Glue job when new backup files arrive, extracting changed records from the backup
explain: AWS DMS CDC mode reads PostgreSQL's Write Ahead Log (WAL replication slots) to capture every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE without adding overhead to the transactional database. Changes are streamed to Amazon S3 or Kinesis in near-real-time. This is the purpose-built AWS service for operational database to data lake replication. RDS event subscriptions only notify on instance-level events (failover, maintenance), not row-level data changes. Glue with JDBC polling can capture new/updated rows using a timestamp column but cannot detect deletes (no tombstone records) and adds SELECT load to the production database. Automated backup-based CDC has high latency (backup frequency) and is complex to implement.

q: A company's Amazon Athena queries on S3 data are slow because they scan entire tables even when queries filter on a specific date column. The data is stored as Parquet files in a flat S3 prefix (no partitioning). What change provides the GREATEST query performance improvement?
options: Reorganize the S3 data into date-based partitions (s3://bucket/table/year=2024/month=04/day=21/) and update the Glue Data Catalog table definition — Athena will use partition pruning to scan only the date-relevant files
Enable Athena query result caching — repeated queries on the same date range will return cached results
Compress the Parquet files using Snappy compression — compressed files scan faster than uncompressed files
Switch from Parquet to ORC format — ORC has better date column predicate pushdown than Parquet
explain: Date-based partitioning is the most impactful optimization for date-filtered queries. Without partitions, Athena scans all files for any date query. With date partitions, Athena's query planner uses partition pruning to identify and scan only the S3 files in the relevant date folders — potentially reducing data scanned by 99.7% for a single day query on 3 years of data. Query result caching helps for repeated identical queries but doesn't speed up new queries or reduce underlying scan costs. Snappy compression reduces file size but doesn't avoid scanning all files. Both Parquet and ORC support predicate pushdown for columns within a file — partitioning is about avoiding files entirely.

q: A data analyst discovers that an AWS Glue ETL job has been running for 6 hours and hasn't completed, while a similar job historically finishes in 45 minutes. The Glue job monitors show normal DPU utilization. What should the data engineer investigate FIRST?
options: Check the Glue job's CloudWatch metrics for data skew — if one partition is processing 90% of the data while others are idle, data skew causes a 'straggler' task that blocks job completion. Use spark.sql.adaptive.enabled to allow adaptive partition merging
Increase the number of DPUs from 10 to 50 — more parallel workers reduce processing time proportionally
Restart the Glue job with a fresh environment — long-running Glue jobs often encounter memory leaks that require a fresh job run
Convert the Glue job from Spark to Python Shell — Python Shell jobs don't have the overhead of Spark cluster initialization
explain: When a Glue job runs far longer than usual with normal DPU utilization, data skew is the most common root cause. Data skew means one Spark partition has a disproportionate amount of data (e.g., a category with 1M records vs others with 1K records). That single 'straggler' partition runs on one executor while all other executors finish and sit idle — causing the job to wait for the straggler. CloudWatch shows normal average utilization even though one task is bottlenecking. Spark AQE (Adaptive Query Execution with spark.sql.adaptive.enabled) can automatically split skewed partitions. Adding DPUs doesn't help if the bottleneck is a single task. Memory leaks are rare in serverless Glue jobs. Python Shell jobs can't handle large-scale distributed processing.

q: A company wants to implement a data mesh architecture where multiple domain teams own and publish their data products. Each team manages their own AWS account. Central data consumers need to discover and access data products across all domain accounts. Which AWS services enable this federated data mesh architecture?
options: Use AWS Lake Formation with cross-account data sharing — domain teams register their S3 data in Lake Formation and share databases/tables to the central consumer account; consumers use Athena or Redshift Spectrum to query data products across all accounts without data movement
Require all domain teams to copy their data to a central S3 bucket in the consumer account before it can be queried
Use Amazon S3 cross-account replication to copy all domain data to a central data lake account, then catalog it in a single Glue Data Catalog
Set up AWS Transit Gateway between all domain accounts and the consumer account to enable direct S3 cross-account network access
explain: AWS Lake Formation cross-account data sharing enables the data mesh pattern without data movement. Domain teams: (1) Register their S3 data in Lake Formation. (2) Grant Lake Formation permissions to the consumer account's IAM principals on specific databases/tables. Consumer teams: (1) Access shared databases/tables through their own Glue Data Catalog. (2) Query using Athena or Redshift Spectrum — data stays in the domain account's S3. This achieves the data mesh goals of domain ownership, central discoverability, and federated access. Copying data to a central account violates data mesh domain ownership. S3 replication creates duplicate costs and stale copies. Transit Gateway is for VPC networking, not S3 data access.

q: A financial company needs to build a streaming analytics pipeline that detects fraudulent transactions in real-time. Transactions arrive via Amazon Kinesis Data Streams at 50,000 events/second. A fraud detection model must score each transaction within 500ms. Complex event processing (detecting patterns across 5 consecutive transactions from the same card) is also required. Which AWS service provides these capabilities?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) — Flink supports low-latency stateful stream processing with time-window operations and complex event processing (CEP) for pattern detection across multiple events, while maintaining per-card state in a managed state store
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with a Lambda transformation function — Lambda scores each transaction individually using the fraud model within the 500ms SLA
Amazon SQS FIFO queues with a consumer Lambda function that reads transactions in batches and detects patterns using a sliding window implemented in DynamoDB
AWS Glue Streaming ETL with micro-batch processing — Glue streams from Kinesis and processes 30-second micro-batches for fraud detection
explain: Complex event processing across 5 consecutive transactions requires stateful stream processing — Apache Flink (via Kinesis Data Analytics) is purpose-built for this. Flink maintains per-card state (last N transactions) in a distributed state store, applies time windows, and uses Flink's CEP library for pattern detection. Flink achieves sub-second latency at 50K events/second. Firehose + Lambda can score individual transactions but cannot detect patterns across 5 consecutive events without complex external state management. SQS FIFO + DynamoDB sliding window is complex, high-latency, and not designed for 50K events/second. Glue streaming uses 30-second micro-batches — 30 seconds is far outside the 500ms requirement.

q: A data engineering team discovers that their Amazon Redshift queries are failing intermittently with 'disk space full' errors even though the cluster has 50% free storage on average. Investigation shows certain complex queries create massive temporary tables during execution. Which MOST effectively resolves this?
options: Enable Redshift workload management (WLM) queue configuration to limit concurrency for memory-intensive queries, add a dedicated WLM queue for large queries with memory allocation limits, and enable Redshift managed storage to automatically scale disk capacity
Increase the Redshift cluster size by adding more nodes — more storage resolves disk full errors permanently
Set a query result size limit in Redshift to prevent any single query from using more than 10GB of temporary storage
Move Redshift to RA3 node type which uses managed storage backed by S3 — temporary tables automatically spill to S3 without disk limits
explain: Disk full errors during complex queries indicate temporary table spillover exceeding node disk capacity. WLM provides two controls: (1) Concurrency limiting prevents multiple large queries from running simultaneously and competing for temp disk. (2) Memory allocation per query limits how much each query can use (and thus how large temp tables can be), causing queries to optimize query plans. Redshift managed storage (for RA3) allows permanent data to spill to S3 but temporary tables still consume node disk. Simply adding nodes increases total cluster storage but the per-query temp space on individual nodes is still bounded. Query result size limits cap results returned to the client but don't limit intermediate temp table sizes during query execution.

q: A company wants to automatically classify all data in their S3 data lake to identify tables containing sensitive data (PII, financial records) before granting access. The classification results should integrate with their access control decisions in AWS Lake Formation. Which AWS service provides automated data classification integrated with Lake Formation?
options: Amazon Macie — enable Macie on S3 buckets to automatically identify PII and sensitive data using ML models; Macie findings can be used to inform Lake Formation tag policies, where sensitive data is tagged and access is restricted accordingly
AWS Glue DataBrew — configure DataBrew to profile all S3 datasets and generate sensitivity reports, then manually update Lake Formation policies based on the reports
Amazon Rekognition — enable Rekognition to scan S3 files for sensitive content in images and documents, then configure S3 event notifications to update Lake Formation permissions
AWS Security Hub — enable Security Hub's data sensitivity standards to automatically classify S3 data and push classification tags to Lake Formation
explain: Amazon Macie uses ML to automatically classify data in S3, detecting PII (names, addresses, SSNs, credit card numbers), financial data, credentials, and other sensitive information. Macie findings identify which S3 objects/buckets contain sensitive data. These findings can inform Lake Formation tag-based access control — administrators apply LF-tags (e.g., DataSensitivity=PII) to Lake Formation registered tables based on Macie's classification, then grant/deny access based on those tags. Glue DataBrew profiles data (statistics, distributions) but is primarily for data quality, not automated sensitivity classification. Rekognition analyzes images, not tabular/text data in Parquet files. Security Hub aggregates security findings but doesn't perform data classification.

q: A company uses Amazon Redshift for their data warehouse. The business intelligence team reports that during business hours (9 AM - 5 PM), the cluster is heavily utilized with queue times up to 10 minutes, while at night the cluster is nearly idle. Which Redshift feature reduces costs while handling peak load?
options: Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling for the WLM queue — Concurrency Scaling automatically adds transient cluster capacity during peak hours at per-second billing, handling burst queries without permanent cluster resizing
Enable Redshift auto-pause — the cluster automatically pauses during low-utilization periods and resumes when queries arrive, eliminating nighttime costs
Migrate from Redshift to Amazon Athena — Athena scales automatically and has no cluster costs during idle periods
Schedule an Amazon EventBridge rule to resize the Redshift cluster to 10x nodes at 8:59 AM and resize back to baseline at 5:01 PM daily
explain: Redshift Concurrency Scaling is designed for burst workload handling. When the main cluster's WLM queue reaches the concurrency limit, additional transient compute clusters automatically spin up to handle burst queries, billed per-second only when active. This eliminates long queue waits during peak hours without permanently sizing the cluster for peak load. Auto-pause works for Serverless or RA3 clusters and pauses after inactivity — excellent for reducing nighttime costs but doesn't help with peak-hour burst capacity. Migrating to Athena is a major architectural change. Scheduled cluster resizing takes several minutes and is an approximation — Concurrency Scaling responds instantly.

q: A company has a legacy on-premises Oracle database that needs to be continuously replicated to Amazon Redshift for real-time reporting. The Oracle database has 500GB of data and receives 10,000 transactions per minute during business hours. Which migration approach minimizes downtime and provides ongoing synchronization?
options: Use AWS DMS for a full load migration followed by ongoing CDC (change data capture) replication from Oracle to Redshift — DMS reads Oracle redo logs for CDC, minimizing production database impact while maintaining sub-minute Redshift synchronization
Use AWS Schema Conversion Tool to convert Oracle schema to Redshift, then write a custom ETL script that queries Oracle with a timestamp filter every minute and upserts changes to Redshift
Schedule a nightly Oracle dump, upload to S3, and use AWS Glue to load the full dataset into Redshift daily — accept up to 24-hour data lag for reporting
Use AWS DataSync to continuously sync Oracle data files to S3, then use Redshift COPY command triggered by S3 event notifications to load new data
explain: AWS DMS is purpose-built for this scenario: (1) Full load phase migrates the 500GB baseline to Redshift during a maintenance window. (2) CDC phase switches to continuous log-based replication using Oracle redo logs — capturing every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE with minimal production Oracle impact (<5% overhead). DMS delivers changes to Redshift with sub-minute latency. Custom timestamp-based ETL cannot detect deletes and adds SELECT load to Oracle. Nightly full load accepts 24-hour lag — incompatible with real-time reporting requirements. Oracle data files aren't accessible as file system objects for DataSync, and Redshift COPY doesn't support CDC/upsert patterns.

q: A company implements a data lakehouse on AWS using Amazon S3 and Apache Iceberg tables. They need to track all data modifications (inserts, updates, deletes) for regulatory compliance, including who changed what data and when. Which feature provides this audit trail?
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for the S3 bucket to log all S3 API calls with user identity and timestamps, and use Apache Iceberg's built-in table metadata (snapshot history and manifest files) to track what data changed in each Iceberg transaction
Enable Amazon Macie on the S3 bucket to monitor data modifications and generate compliance reports on data access patterns
Use Amazon S3 Object Lock in governance mode on all Iceberg data files to prevent modifications and ensure data integrity
Configure Amazon S3 Versioning to keep all versions of modified Iceberg data files, providing a complete modification history
explain: A complete data modification audit trail requires two complementary sources: (1) CloudTrail data events capture who performed each S3 API operation (PutObject, DeleteObject) with IAM identity, timestamp, and source IP. (2) Apache Iceberg's snapshot-based architecture maintains a complete history of all table states — each INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE creates a new snapshot with metadata about which data files changed, allowing point-in-time queries and audit of data evolution. Together, these provide a complete chain of custody. Macie classifies data but doesn't track modification history. Object Lock prevents modifications but the requirement is to track modifications (Iceberg-managed tables require write capability). S3 Versioning keeps old object versions but Iceberg creates new files rather than overwriting — versioning helps but doesn't capture the semantic data changes.

q: A data team uses AWS Glue to catalog data in Amazon S3. After updating a large dataset with new files, Athena queries return stale data — the new files are not appearing in query results. What is the MOST likely cause and fix?
options: The Glue Data Catalog's partition metadata is stale — run a Glue crawler or use the MSCK REPAIR TABLE command in Athena to discover and register new S3 partitions that were added since the last catalog update
Athena has a 1-hour query result cache that is returning old results — disable caching in the Athena workgroup settings to force fresh data reads
The S3 data files are in a 'write pending' state due to eventual consistency — wait 15 minutes for S3 to propagate the new files before querying
The Glue ETL job didn't finish writing the files — check the Glue job run status and wait for successful completion before querying
explain: When new Parquet/CSV files are added to S3 partitions outside of Glue ETL jobs (e.g., direct S3 upload or another pipeline), the Glue Data Catalog is not automatically updated. Athena queries the metadata in the Glue catalog to find which S3 paths to scan — if new partitions aren't registered in the catalog, Athena doesn't know they exist. MSCK REPAIR TABLE scans S3 to discover new partitions and registers them. A Glue crawler also discovers and registers new partitions. Athena query result caching caches query results by SQL hash but expired caches force fresh reads. S3 is strongly consistent since December 2020 — no 15-minute wait needed. The question specifies successful file updates, so Glue job status is not the issue.

q: A data engineering team needs to implement a medallion architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) in their AWS data lake. Bronze stores raw data, Silver stores cleaned/transformed data, and Gold stores aggregated business metrics. Which AWS services best implement each layer?
options: Bronze: Amazon S3 (raw, ingested as-is from Kinesis Firehose); Silver: Amazon S3 (Parquet via Glue ETL with data quality checks); Gold: Amazon Redshift (aggregated metrics for BI tools) or Amazon S3 (Parquet) queried via Athena — all layers cataloged in Glue Data Catalog with Lake Formation governance
Bronze: Amazon RDS; Silver: Amazon EMR; Gold: Amazon DynamoDB — each layer uses a different database service for isolation
Bronze: Amazon S3 Glacier (low cost for raw data); Silver: S3 Standard-IA; Gold: S3 Standard — storage class reflects data maturity
Bronze: Amazon Kinesis (data streams); Silver: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (processing); Gold: Amazon QuickSight (visualization) — streaming-only architecture
explain: The medallion architecture maps naturally to AWS services: Bronze (raw landing) uses S3 because it's the cheapest, most durable object store and accepts any format. Silver (cleaned) uses S3 with Parquet (columnar, compressed) after Glue ETL applies schema validation, deduplication, and data quality checks. Gold (business aggregates) uses Redshift for BI tool connectivity, pre-aggregated queries, and sub-second response times. Alternatively, Gold can be Parquet in S3 queried with Athena. The Glue Data Catalog provides metadata across all layers; Lake Formation provides governance. Using RDS/DynamoDB for different layers breaks the data lake paradigm. Glacier for Bronze is wrong — Bronze data is actively read for Silver processing. Kinesis is for streaming ingestion, not batch storage.

q: A company uses AWS Glue to run ETL jobs that process data from S3 and load it into Amazon Redshift. The team needs to implement data quality checks ensuring no NULL values exist in key columns and that value ranges are within expected bounds before loading into Redshift. Which AWS service integrates natively with Glue for this purpose?
options: AWS Glue Data Quality — configure DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language) rules directly in the Glue ETL job or as a separate Glue Data Quality evaluation, defining constraints like 'IsComplete(customer_id)' and 'ColumnValues(price) between 0 and 10000', automatically blocking Redshift load on rule failure
Amazon DataZone — configure DataZone data quality profiles to validate Glue job outputs before Redshift loading
Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor — configure Model Monitor to evaluate Glue ETL output data quality and trigger Redshift load only when quality metrics pass
AWS Config — create a custom Config rule that evaluates Glue job output S3 files for data quality before Redshift COPY operations
explain: AWS Glue Data Quality is natively integrated with Glue ETL jobs. DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language) provides a declarative way to define data quality rules (completeness, range checks, uniqueness, referential integrity). Rules can be embedded directly in Glue ETL scripts or run as standalone evaluations in the Glue workflow. When rules fail, the job can be configured to fail (blocking bad data from reaching Redshift) or warn (log issues and continue). This is tightly integrated with Glue's DynamicFrame API. Amazon DataZone is for data discovery and governance catalogs. SageMaker Model Monitor evaluates ML model prediction quality, not ETL data quality. AWS Config evaluates AWS resource configuration, not data file contents.

q: A retail company wants to analyze customer purchase behavior in real-time to power personalized product recommendations during the shopping session. Transaction events are published to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. The recommendation engine needs enriched events that include customer profile data from Amazon DynamoDB. Which approach enriches streaming events with customer profile data in real-time?
options: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) with a DynamoDB lookup join — Flink reads the transaction stream and enriches each event by performing an async DynamoDB lookup for the customer profile, combining both data sources in-memory before outputting enriched events
Write all Kinesis events to S3 using Firehose, use Glue to join with DynamoDB export files nightly, and serve recommendations from the resulting enriched S3 dataset
Configure Kinesis Data Firehose with a Lambda transformation that looks up DynamoDB for each event before writing to S3, then use Athena for real-time recommendation queries
Enable DynamoDB Streams on the customer profile table and merge both streams in Amazon SQS, then process merged messages in Lambda to generate recommendations
explain: Apache Flink in Kinesis Data Analytics supports async lookups to external systems (DynamoDB, RDS) as a stream enrichment pattern. Each transaction event triggers an async DynamoDB GetItem call for the customer profile. Flink's async I/O API handles this efficiently without blocking the stream, achieving high throughput with low latency enrichment. This is exactly the streaming join-with-reference-data pattern. Nightly batch enrichment doesn't support real-time personalization during a shopping session. Firehose + Lambda transformation can do DynamoDB lookups but Firehose buffers before S3 delivery (no real-time serving). Merging Kinesis and DynamoDB Streams in SQS loses ordering and doesn't correlate events with profiles correctly.

q: A data team uses AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a complex data pipeline: Glue ETL → Redshift load → email notification. During testing, the Glue step fails, but Step Functions retries it 3 times and then sends the email notification anyway. The team wants the pipeline to stop and alert on failure without sending a success notification. How should the Step Functions state machine be corrected?
options: Add a Catch block on the Glue task state that transitions to an SNS-based failure notification state on all errors, configure the Retry policy to stop after 3 retries, and only connect the success path to the Redshift load → email success state
Set the Glue task maxAttempts to 0 to disable retries so failures surface immediately
Add a Choice state after the Glue task that checks the output for error indicators and routes to a failure branch
Enable Step Functions Express workflows instead of Standard — Express workflows propagate errors differently and won't continue to notification on failure
explain: Step Functions error handling requires: (1) A Retry policy on the Glue task defining the retry count and backoff (after 3 retries, the task enters a failed state). (2) A Catch block on the Glue task that catches States.ALL errors and transitions to a failure notification state (SNS or SES). The success path (Glue → Redshift → success email) is only followed when Glue succeeds. Without a Catch block, Step Functions' default behavior is to propagate the error to the execution level — but the pipeline was apparently continuing to the email step, suggesting incorrect state machine transitions. Setting maxAttempts to 0 just skips retries. A Choice state checking output requires the Glue task to complete (failed tasks don't have success output). Express vs Standard doesn't change error propagation fundamentally.

q: A company's data platform team wants to implement data lineage tracking to understand how data flows from source systems through transformations to final reports. When a data quality issue is discovered in a report, they need to trace it back to the source. Which AWS service provides automated data lineage tracking?
options: Amazon DataZone — DataZone automatically tracks data lineage across AWS data services (Glue, Redshift, Athena), showing the complete transformation path from S3 source files through ETL jobs to final tables and reports in a visual lineage graph
AWS CloudTrail — enable CloudTrail data events for all data services to capture the complete chain of S3 reads and writes, then query CloudTrail to reconstruct data lineage manually
AWS Glue Data Catalog — the catalog's table history shows which Glue jobs created or modified each table, providing implicit lineage through job execution records
Amazon Macie — enable Macie on all S3 buckets to detect sensitive data movement between buckets, which provides lineage for sensitive data flows
explain: Amazon DataZone provides automated data lineage as a first-class feature. It integrates with AWS data services and automatically captures how data flows through Glue ETL jobs, Redshift transformations, and Athena queries. The visual lineage graph shows upstream (data sources) and downstream (consumers) for any data asset, enabling root cause analysis when quality issues are discovered. CloudTrail data events capture API-level S3 access but don't capture semantic data transformations (which columns were computed from which sources). Glue Data Catalog shows which job created a table but doesn't track column-level lineage or multi-hop transformations. Macie tracks sensitive data detection, not data flow lineage.

q: A company wants to enable analysts to run SQL queries on their Amazon S3 data lake without provisioning or managing infrastructure. The data is stored in Parquet format, partitioned by date. Queries typically scan 3-7 days of data. Which service requires the LEAST infrastructure management while providing acceptable query performance?
options: Amazon Athena — fully serverless, queries S3 data directly using standard SQL, billed per-TB scanned, no cluster management required. Partition pruning on date partitions ensures only 3-7 days of Parquet files are scanned per query
Amazon Redshift Spectrum — configure a Redshift cluster with Spectrum to query S3 data in place, providing familiar Redshift SQL and fast query performance
Amazon EMR Serverless — deploy an EMR Serverless application running PySpark, submit SQL queries via Spark SQL without managing clusters
Amazon OpenSearch Service — index all Parquet data into OpenSearch for low-latency SQL-like queries on the data lake
explain: Amazon Athena is the most infrastructure-free SQL query service for S3 data lakes: zero cluster management, zero provisioning, pay-per-query (per-TB scanned). With Parquet format and date-based partitioning, Athena uses partition pruning to scan only relevant data, minimizing cost and latency. Analysts write standard SQL and run queries immediately. Redshift Spectrum requires managing an underlying Redshift cluster (provisioning, scaling, ongoing cluster cost). EMR Serverless is serverless but requires Spark knowledge rather than standard SQL. OpenSearch is excellent for log search and analytics but requires ingesting all Parquet data (not querying S3 in place), adding ingestion overhead and storage duplication.

q: A company migrates 100TB of historical data from an on-premises Hadoop HDFS cluster to Amazon S3. The migration must complete in 5 days, and the on-premises network bandwidth is limited to 1 Gbps. Doing the math: 100TB / 5 days = 20TB/day = ~230MB/s — close to the network limit. Which approach ensures reliable, efficient migration within the timeline?
options: Use AWS DataSync with an on-premises DataSync agent installed near the HDFS cluster — DataSync automatically parallelizes transfers, uses TCP optimization for maximum throughput, verifies data integrity with checksums, and handles network interruptions with automatic retry
Write a custom Python script using boto3 that reads HDFS files and uploads to S3 using multipart upload, running on multiple EC2 instances in a VPN-connected VPC
Use AWS Snowball Edge — ship 10 Snowball Edge devices (10TB each), load data, ship back to AWS, and import to S3
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with an HDFS source connector to stream data from HDFS to S3 continuously
explain: AWS DataSync is the purpose-built service for large-scale file migration to S3. It: (1) Automatically parallelizes transfers across multiple streams to saturate available bandwidth. (2) Performs TCP-level optimization to maximize throughput on WAN links. (3) Verifies integrity with checksums during and after transfer. (4) Handles interruptions and resumes automatically. At 230MB/s sustained for 5 days, DataSync can complete 100TB within the timeline. A custom Python script requires development effort and doesn't have DataSync's optimization. Snowball Edge: 10 devices × 10TB capacity are insufficient (need 10× 10TB = 100TB, but device capacity and return logistics take 7-10 days — too long). Firehose doesn't have an HDFS source connector.

q: A company's analytics team uses Amazon Athena to query sensitive customer data in S3. The data includes encrypted PII columns. The security team wants to ensure Athena queries are limited to authorized analysts, query history is logged for compliance, and results are stored in an encrypted S3 location. Which configuration achieves all three requirements?
options: Create an Athena workgroup for analytics team with IAM policies restricting access to the workgroup and specific Glue tables, configure the workgroup to enforce query result location in an encrypted S3 bucket (SSE-KMS), enable CloudTrail to log all Athena API calls including query history
Use Amazon Macie on the query result S3 bucket to detect and redact PII before analysts download results from S3
Deploy Athena behind an API Gateway that requires authentication, implement a Lambda authorizer to validate analyst credentials, and configure API Gateway access logging for query history
Enable Athena SAML federation to require corporate SSO for Athena console access, and use S3 server-side encryption for query results
explain: Athena workgroups provide all three controls: (1) Authorization — IAM policies on the workgroup restrict which analysts can submit queries and which Glue tables they can access (combined with Lake Formation table permissions). (2) Encrypted results — workgroup settings can enforce a specific S3 result location with required SSE-KMS encryption (analysts cannot override this). (3) Audit — CloudTrail logs all Athena API calls (StartQueryExecution, GetQueryResults) with user identity, query text, and timestamps. Macie detects PII in results but doesn't restrict who can run queries. API Gateway adds unnecessary infrastructure. SAML federation handles authentication (who is the user?) but the workgroup handles authorization (what can they do?).

q: A company's data team uses Amazon Redshift for analytics. During peak hours, long-running reports block interactive analyst queries in the same WLM queue, causing 5-10 minute wait times. The team wants interactive queries to respond within 30 seconds regardless of report load. Which Redshift feature directly addresses this?
options: Configure separate WLM queues: a dedicated 'interactive' queue (high priority, memory allocation, short query max duration of 60 seconds) and a 'batch reports' queue (lower priority, separate memory allocation) — route queries to queues based on user group or query label
Enable Redshift Serverless to automatically route interactive queries to fast, auto-scaling compute separate from the reporting cluster
Enable Redshift auto-analyze to keep table statistics current, which speeds up query planning for interactive queries
Use Amazon Athena for all interactive queries instead of Redshift — Athena's serverless architecture doesn't have queue contention issues
explain: Redshift WLM (Workload Management) queue configuration directly solves query prioritization. By creating separate queues for interactive and batch workloads with defined memory allocations, the interactive queue gets dedicated resources. A 60-second maximum query duration prevents runaway interactives from consuming resources; a query label or user group routes each query automatically to the right queue. Concurrency Scaling adds capacity for burst demand. Redshift Serverless auto-scales but doesn't inherently solve the prioritization problem between query types. Auto-analyze improves statistics but doesn't change queue resource allocation. Migrating to Athena is a major change that doesn't address the existing Redshift investment.

q: A retail company's marketing team wants to send personalized promotional emails within 1 hour of a customer abandoning their shopping cart. The cart abandonment events are stored in Amazon DynamoDB (with TTL for cleanup). What architecture detects cart abandonment events and triggers the email workflow?
options: Enable DynamoDB Streams on the cart table, trigger a Lambda function on stream records to detect items where cart has been inactive for 1 hour, then publish to Amazon SNS or SES for email sending — use DynamoDB TTL expiry events in Streams to trigger the workflow when the TTL item expires
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to export DynamoDB changes to S3, run an hourly Glue job to identify abandoned carts from S3 data, and trigger emails via SES
Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule every 5 minutes that triggers a Lambda function to scan the entire DynamoDB cart table for abandoned carts using a table scan

Use Amazon SQS FIFO with a visibility timeout of 1 hour — when a cart item is added, add a message to SQS; if the cart is purchased, delete the SQS message; if not purchased, the message becomes visible after 1 hour and triggers email
explain: The SQS visibility timeout pattern elegantly solves this: when a cart item is added, insert an SQS message. If the customer checks out, delete the corresponding SQS message (cancellation). If the customer abandons the cart, the SQS message becomes visible after the 1-hour visibility timeout and triggers the email Lambda consumer. This is the simplest, most reliable pattern for 'do X if event Y doesn't happen within N minutes.' DynamoDB Streams detect changes in real-time but don't handle the 1-hour delay elegantly without additional time-tracking logic. Glue hourly jobs have up to 1-hour lag and Firehose adds S3 delivery delay. Table scans every 5 minutes scale poorly and add read capacity consumption.

q: A company stores analytics data in Amazon Redshift and needs to share specific aggregated tables with a business partner's AWS account for collaborative reporting, without sharing raw underlying data or giving the partner direct access to their Redshift cluster. Which Redshift feature enables this?
options: Amazon Redshift Data Sharing — create a datashare in the source cluster containing only the specific tables/views to share, grant access to the partner account, and the partner creates an Amazon Redshift consumer cluster that queries the shared data in real-time without data movement or duplication
Export the specific tables to S3 as Parquet files and share the S3 bucket with the partner account's IAM role using a cross-account bucket policy
Create an Amazon Redshift read replica in the partner's AWS account and enable replication for only the approved tables
Use AWS DataSync to transfer the approved tables from Redshift to an RDS PostgreSQL instance in the partner's account for reporting
explain: Amazon Redshift Data Sharing provides live, read-only access to specific databases, schemas, or tables across AWS accounts and regions without data movement. The producer cluster creates a datashare containing exactly the tables/views to share. The consumer cluster accesses the shared data in real-time, seeing current data without any ETL or replication. Raw tables not included in the datashare are invisible to the consumer. This perfectly meets the requirement — partner sees only aggregated tables, not raw data. Exporting to S3 creates a static snapshot requiring scheduled updates and doesn't provide real-time data. Redshift doesn't support cross-account read replicas. DataSync migrates files (not databases) and RDS doesn't provide the same Redshift-native experience.

q: A company wants to build an event-driven data pipeline where new files arriving in Amazon S3 automatically trigger data processing. Files arrive 24/7 at irregular intervals (10 files/hour during off-peak, 500 files/hour during peak). The processing must scale automatically and complete within 5 minutes of file arrival. Which architecture is MOST appropriate?
options: Configure S3 Event Notifications to publish to Amazon SQS, create an AWS Lambda function triggered by SQS with appropriate concurrency limits to process each file — SQS buffers the events during peak, Lambda scales automatically, and SQS visibility timeout handles retry on failure
Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule every 5 minutes to trigger a Lambda function that lists new S3 files and processes them in batch
Configure Kinesis Data Firehose with an S3 source to detect new files and trigger a Glue streaming job for processing
Use AWS Glue with a scheduled trigger every 15 minutes to run an ETL job that scans S3 for new files and processes them in batch
explain: S3 → SQS → Lambda is the canonical event-driven file processing pattern on AWS. S3 event notifications deliver each new file arrival to SQS within seconds. Lambda auto-scales to process messages concurrently (up to 1,000 concurrent invocations), handling the 500 files/hour peak. SQS provides buffering during bursts and decouples S3 from Lambda, ensuring no events are lost if Lambda briefly can't keep up. Visibility timeout provides automatic retry on processing failure. Total file-to-completion time is typically 30-90 seconds — well within the 5-minute requirement. Scheduled polling (EventBridge or Glue every 5-15 minutes) adds latency and processes files in batches rather than individually. Firehose doesn't trigger on existing S3 files.

q: A company processes clickstream data from their website using Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. During traffic spikes (product launches), the stream receives 10x normal volume and data starts falling behind. The processing Lambda function cannot scale fast enough. Which approach prevents data loss during traffic spikes?
options: Increase the number of Kinesis shards to provide more throughput capacity (each shard handles 1MB/s input, 2MB/s output) and adjust Lambda's event source mapping batch size and parallelization factor to process more records concurrently per shard
Enable Kinesis Data Firehose on the same stream as a secondary consumer — Firehose will store overflow records to S3 that Lambda missed
Increase the Kinesis stream retention period from 24 hours to 7 days — this provides a larger buffer so Lambda can catch up after the spike
Enable Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out for the Lambda consumer — Enhanced Fan-Out provides dedicated throughput per consumer, ensuring Lambda always gets all records
explain: The bottleneck is insufficient shard throughput. Each Kinesis shard supports 1MB/s or 1,000 records/s input. If traffic spikes 10x, the stream needs 10x more shards. Additionally, Lambda's concurrency is bounded by the number of shards × parallelization factor. Increasing shards and Lambda parallelization factor allows proportional scaling of both ingestion and processing. Firehose as a secondary consumer stores records to S3 but doesn't help Lambda process them — it's a separate data path. Increasing retention from 24 to 7 hours allows catch-up but risks hours of data delay and doesn't solve the throughput bottleneck. Enhanced Fan-Out provides dedicated per-consumer throughput from the shard (instead of shared) but if the shard is at capacity for input, the bottleneck is still the shard count.

q: A company's Amazon Redshift RA3 cluster is hitting storage limits but query performance is good. The team wants to offload older, less-accessed data to reduce Redshift costs while keeping it queryable. Which approach achieves this WITHOUT losing query capability?
options: Use Redshift Spectrum: unload older data from Redshift to Amazon S3 in Parquet format, drop the Redshift tables for that data, create Redshift external tables via Glue Data Catalog pointing to the S3 data — Spectrum queries the S3 data at query time while recent data remains in Redshift
Enable Redshift automated snapshots and restore older data to a smaller Redshift cluster when needed, terminating the secondary cluster after the query
Move older data to Amazon DynamoDB and join DynamoDB tables with Redshift tables using a cross-service ETL pipeline before each query
Compress older Redshift tables using DEFLATE encoding to reduce storage footprint by 80% while keeping data in Redshift
explain: Redshift Spectrum is the purpose-built solution for hot/cold data tiering: recent data in Redshift (fast, high cost), historical data in S3 (slower, cheap). Unloading old data to S3 Parquet frees Redshift storage. Redshift external tables via the Glue Data Catalog allow Spectrum to query S3 data using the same Redshift SQL. Queries can JOIN Redshift tables with Spectrum (S3) tables. This maintains full query capability while dramatically reducing Redshift storage cost. Snapshot + restore clusters adds significant latency (cluster restore takes 10+ minutes). DynamoDB doesn't integrate directly with Redshift for SQL joins. DEFLATE compression reduces storage 30-50% (not 80%) and doesn't solve the fundamental capacity issue for long-term cost reduction.

q: A data engineering team needs to track the progress and status of all their data pipeline jobs (Glue, EMR, Lambda, DMS) in a single monitoring dashboard. Pipeline failures must alert the on-call engineer within 5 minutes. Which monitoring architecture achieves this?
options: Enable CloudWatch metrics for all services (Glue, EMR, Lambda, DMS), create CloudWatch composite alarms that aggregate job success/failure metrics, configure SNS for 5-minute alerting, and build a CloudWatch Dashboard visualizing pipeline health across all services
Deploy AWS CloudTrail for all services to capture API events, build a custom Lambda that parses CloudTrail logs every minute, aggregates pipeline status, and sends SMS alerts via SNS on failures
Use Amazon Managed Grafana connected to Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus — deploy Prometheus exporters on all data processing services for unified metrics collection
Create separate monitoring Lambda functions for each service that poll service-specific APIs every minute for job status, storing results in DynamoDB for dashboard queries
explain: AWS CloudWatch is the native, zero-configuration monitoring service for all AWS services. Glue, EMR, Lambda, and DMS all publish native metrics (job success, failure, runtime) to CloudWatch automatically. Composite alarms allow combining alerts from multiple service metrics into unified pipeline health indicators. SNS integration provides 5-minute alerting. CloudWatch Dashboards visualize all metrics in a single pane of glass — no extra infrastructure. CloudTrail is for API auditing, not real-time metrics monitoring; parsing CloudTrail logs for job status adds latency. Grafana + Prometheus is valid for self-managed infrastructure but is over-engineered when CloudWatch natively receives all these metrics. Per-service polling Lambdas are complex and expensive.

q: A company wants to implement row-level security in Amazon Redshift so that each sales representative can only query rows corresponding to their own sales region. The region is determined by their IAM Identity Center (SSO) username. Which Redshift feature implements this?
options: Create a Redshift row-level security (RLS) policy that filters rows based on the current_user function matching the sales_rep column — 'CREATE RLS POLICY rep_policy USING (sales_rep = current_user)' — attach this policy to the table and enable it
Create separate Redshift schemas per sales region and grant each IAM user access only to their region's schema
Use Amazon Redshift Spectrum with Lake Formation row filter security policies applied to the underlying S3 data
Create database views per sales rep that include a WHERE clause filtering to their region, and grant each rep access only to their view
explain: Amazon Redshift natively supports row-level security (RLS) policies. An RLS policy defines a filter expression (USING clause) that is automatically appended to every query against the protected table. Using 'current_user' allows the policy to dynamically filter based on the connected database user. When IAM Identity Center users connect through Redshift, their identity maps to a database user. This enforces the restriction transparently — reps cannot bypass it by writing their own WHERE clauses. Separate schemas require managing N schemas as the sales team grows — high operational overhead. Lake Formation RLS works for S3-based data via Spectrum but isn't the native Redshift feature for this. Per-rep views require N view management and are not dynamic.

q: A logistics company needs to correlate IoT GPS location data (from trucks, 100K records/minute) with geofencing rules (stored in DynamoDB) to detect when trucks enter or exit designated zones, triggering alerts. Which streaming architecture achieves this in near-real-time?
options: Ingest GPS data into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, use Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink) with a DynamoDB enrichment to load geofence zones as a static table, perform geospatial overlap detection using Flink's geo-library or a custom UDF, output match events to SNS for alerting
Batch GPS records in Kinesis Firehose to S3 every 5 minutes, run a Lambda function that loads the 5-minute batch and checks each record against DynamoDB geofences, sending alerts for matches
Write GPS records directly to DynamoDB, create a DynamoDB Stream trigger with Lambda that loads geofence rules and checks each GPS record for zone entry/exit
Use Amazon Location Service to store geofences and device positions, configure Location Service tracker events to trigger EventBridge notifications when devices enter or exit geofences
explain: Amazon Location Service is the purpose-built AWS service for geofencing and device tracking. It natively supports: (1) Storing geofence collections (zones). (2) Tracking device positions (GPS updates). (3) Automatically evaluating device position against geofences and generating ENTER/EXIT events. (4) Delivering these events to Amazon EventBridge for alerting (SNS, Lambda). This eliminates the need for custom geospatial computation code. Kinesis Data Analytics with Flink can do custom geospatial processing but requires developing custom UDFs or geo-library integration. Firehose 5-minute batch processing exceeds the near-real-time requirement. Direct DynamoDB with Lambda at 100K records/minute would exhaust Lambda concurrency and DynamoDB throughput.

q: A company's AWS Glue job processes data from an Amazon S3 source with 1 million small files (avg 5KB each). The job runs for 4+ hours but only processes 5GB of data. What is the PRIMARY cause of poor performance?
options: The small file problem — Spark creates one task per file, resulting in 1 million tasks with high overhead (task startup, JVM serialization, S3 API calls per file). Use Glue's groupFiles option or S3DistCp to consolidate small files into fewer large files before processing
The S3 bucket has too many objects — request an S3 quota increase for objects per bucket to improve listing performance
The Glue job needs more DPUs — 1 million files require at least 1 million DPUs to process in parallel
S3 Transfer Acceleration is not enabled — enable it to improve throughput for millions of small file reads
explain: The small file problem is a notorious Spark/Glue performance anti-pattern. With 1 million 5KB files, Spark creates 1 million tasks. Each task incurs: S3 API call overhead (~10ms), JVM task initialization, shuffle overhead. Total overhead dominates actual processing time (processing 5KB of data takes microseconds, but task setup takes milliseconds). AWS Glue's groupFiles feature consolidates small files into larger logical partitions, reducing task count dramatically. Alternatively, use S3DistCp or Glue to compact files before processing. Adding more DPUs doesn't help — the bottleneck is task management overhead, not compute. S3 quota increases and S3 Transfer Acceleration don't address the small-file task overhead issue.

q: A financial company must ensure that all data transformations on customer data are reproducible — given the same input, the same transformation code must produce the same output. Regulators require the ability to re-run any historical transformation to verify results. Which approach meets the reproducibility requirement?
options: Use AWS Glue with version-controlled scripts in AWS CodeCommit, store input data with S3 Object Lock (preserving immutable inputs), tag each Glue job run with the script version and input S3 location, and store job run metadata (parameters, version) in AWS Glue job run history — allowing any historical transformation to be re-run with the same inputs and code
Enable AWS Glue job bookmarks to track processed input files — bookmarks ensure each file is processed exactly once, preventing duplicate processing
Use Amazon Redshift with automated snapshots — snapshots capture the data warehouse state at each point in time, allowing regulators to review historical transformation outputs
Store all raw input data and transformation outputs in Amazon DynamoDB with a timestamp sort key — querying by timestamp allows retrieval of any historical transformation input/output pair
explain: Reproducibility requires: (1) Immutable inputs — S3 Object Lock preserves the exact input data that was processed. (2) Version-controlled code — CodeCommit tags ensure the exact script version used is preserved. (3) Run metadata — Glue job run history records what inputs, code version, and parameters were used. Together, a regulator can retrieve the script version, point it at the immutable S3 input, and re-run to verify output. Glue job bookmarks track processed files to prevent reprocessing — they don't help reproducibility. Redshift snapshots capture database state but don't preserve transformation code or inputs for re-execution. DynamoDB stores inputs/outputs but doesn't capture the transformation code or ensure inputs are immutable.

q: A company uses Amazon QuickSight to provide business intelligence dashboards to 500 users. Some executives need dashboards with sub-second refresh and real-time data. Other analysts need weekly reporting. Which QuickSight features address both use cases cost-effectively?
options: Use QuickSight SPICE for weekly reporting dashboards (fast cached queries, optimized for large datasets) and configure Direct Query mode for executive real-time dashboards connected to Amazon Redshift or Athena — SPICE imports data on a schedule while Direct Query always reads the latest data
Purchase QuickSight Enterprise Edition for all 500 users and enable all features — Enterprise provides unlimited SPICE and real-time data for all users
Use QuickSight with Amazon ElastiCache as a caching layer — ElastiCache caches QuickSight query results and invalidates cache every second for real-time dashboards
Create separate QuickSight accounts for executives (real-time) and analysts (batch) to optimize each use case independently
explain: QuickSight supports two data access modes per dataset: SPICE (Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine) imports data into a managed in-memory store, providing fast queries for large datasets with scheduled refresh (hourly, daily, weekly). Direct Query mode bypasses SPICE and queries the underlying data source (Redshift, Athena, RDS) in real-time for always-current data. Using SPICE for analyst dashboards (weekly refresh is sufficient) and Direct Query for executive real-time dashboards optimizes cost (SPICE capacity isn't used for all users). Buying Enterprise for all 500 users may be over-provisioning. ElastiCache doesn't integrate with QuickSight. Separate accounts are unnecessary — modes are per-dataset in one account.

q: A company wants to implement a unified data catalog across their entire AWS organization (20 accounts) so that data scientists in any account can discover available datasets. Datasets contain metadata like schema, lineage, and data quality scores. Which AWS service provides this organization-wide data discovery?
options: Amazon DataZone — create a DataZone domain that spans all 20 accounts, enable data producers to publish data assets to the DataZone catalog, and allow data consumers in any account to discover, subscribe to, and request access to datasets through the DataZone business catalog
AWS Glue Data Catalog with cross-account access — share Glue catalogs from each account to a central account using Lake Formation cross-account grants, building a federated catalog
Create a central AWS account with a custom-built data catalog application on EC2 that stores metadata from all 20 accounts in a PostgreSQL RDS database
Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to store dataset metadata (schema, location) from all accounts in a central account with standard SSM cross-account access
explain: Amazon DataZone is the purpose-built AWS data management service for enterprise-scale data cataloging, discovery, and governance. It supports multi-account organizations through a DataZone domain, enabling: (1) Data producers in any account to publish data assets with business metadata, lineage, schema, and quality scores. (2) A unified searchable business catalog accessible to data consumers in all accounts. (3) Governed access requests and subscriptions to datasets. (4) Integration with existing Glue Data Catalog metadata. Glue Data Catalog with Lake Formation cross-account grants provides technical metadata sharing but lacks the business catalog, subscription workflows, and data product management that DataZone provides. Custom-built catalogs add significant development and maintenance overhead. SSM Parameter Store is for configuration data, not structured data cataloging.

q: A company needs to validate that their Kinesis Data Streams producer applications are not dropping records due to throttling. During traffic peaks, they suspect some PutRecord calls are failing with ProvisionedThroughputExceededException. Which monitoring setup detects this issue?
options: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the Kinesis metric 'WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded' — this metric counts the number of records rejected due to throughput limits, indicating producers are hitting shard capacity limits and need either more shards or exponential backoff retry logic
Enable Kinesis Data Streams enhanced monitoring with shard-level metrics and create alarms on the 'GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds' metric — high iterator age indicates records are being dropped
Query AWS CloudTrail for 'ProvisionedThroughputExceededException' error events from Kinesis to count rejected PutRecord calls
Enable Kinesis Data Firehose on the stream — Firehose automatically buffers records when throughput limits are exceeded, preventing data loss without needing additional monitoring
explain: The Kinesis metric 'WriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded' directly measures the number of records/bytes rejected because they exceeded the shard's write throughput limit (1MB/s or 1,000 records/s per shard). A non-zero value on this metric confirms that producers are hitting capacity limits. This is the purpose-built metric for detecting write-side throttling. The remedy is: more shards, or implement exponential backoff retry in producers. IteratorAgeMilliseconds measures consumer lag (how far behind consumers are reading) — not write-side dropped records. CloudTrail captures API-level errors but Kinesis SDK errors are typically returned to the client, not logged to CloudTrail. Adding Firehose doesn't help if records are being dropped at the PutRecord level before Firehose reads them.

q: A company stores JSON event data in Amazon S3 and runs frequent aggregation queries using Amazon Athena. The queries always filter on 'event_type' and aggregate 'revenue' across 'customer_id'. The current scan costs are high because all columns in every JSON file are scanned. What TWO changes provide the GREATEST cost reduction?
options: Convert from JSON to Parquet format (enables columnar reads — only event_type, revenue, and customer_id columns are read instead of all JSON fields) AND partition the data by event_type (enables partition pruning, skipping files for event types not in the query filter)
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the S3 bucket AND enable Athena query result caching with 1-hour TTL
Compress JSON files with gzip AND increase Athena query concurrency quota
Move data to Amazon Redshift AND replace Athena with Redshift SQL queries for all aggregations
explain: Two changes provide maximum Athena cost reduction (billed per-TB scanned): (1) Parquet format — Athena only reads the specific columns needed for a query (columnar storage). For a query using 3 of 50 columns, this reduces data scanned by ~94%. (2) Partitioning by event_type — Athena uses partition pruning to skip all S3 files in partitions not matching the filter, reducing files scanned proportionally. Together, these can reduce scan costs by 95%+. S3 Intelligent-Tiering reduces storage cost but not Athena scan cost. Gzip compression reduces file size (smaller bytes scanned) but JSON remains row-oriented (all fields still scanned per row). Moving to Redshift solves the problem differently but is a major architectural change.
